description: Elderly F, bilateral arm rash, recently started new BP med (likely ACE-I)
transcript:
Dr. Alvarez: Hi hon, I'm Dr. Alvarez. What's going on today?
Patient: Well, I don't even know if I should be here, my neighbor told me I should come in, she's a nurse, well, she was a nurse, she's retired now, she worked at Cedars for thirty years if you can believe it, anyway, I've had this rash.
Dr. Alvarez: Where's the rash?
Patient: On my arm. Well, both arms now. It started on the one and then it went to the other. I thought it was from the garden, I was pulling weeds Saturday, but then I remembered I wore gloves, the good ones, the ones my daughter got me from that place in Pasadena.
Dr. Alvarez: Is it itchy?
Patient: Oh, terribly. I've been putting that cream on it, the pink stuff —
Dr. Alvarez: Calamine?
Patient: Yes, that's it. It helps a little. Not really.
Dr. Alvarez: Any new soaps, detergents, lotions?
Patient: No, I use the same everything for years.
Dr. Alvarez: New medications?
Patient: They started me on something for my blood pressure last month. I don't remember the name. It starts with an L I think.
Dr. Alvarez: Any trouble breathing, swelling of the lips or tongue, fever?
Patient: No, nothing like that.
Dr. Alvarez: Anywhere else on the body?
Patient: A little on my belly. Not much.
Dr. Alvarez: Let me take a look.

Physical exam (dictated): General: elderly female, well-appearing, in no distress, pleasant and conversant. HEENT: no facial swelling, no oral lesions, no tongue swelling. Skin: erythematous, slightly raised, blanching rash with small papules scattered over the bilateral volar forearms extending to the antecubital fossae, a few similar lesions over the lower abdomen, no vesicles, no bullae, no target lesions, no mucosal involvement, no petechiae. No lymphadenopathy. Cardiac regular. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, non-tender. No wheezing, no stridor.

groundTruth.notes: Likely ACE-I-induced drug eruption (the BP med 'starts with L'). Anaphylaxis red flags verbally cleared (no airway/lip/tongue/breathing). Risk axis depends on whether physician stops the ACE-I (= adjusting existing Rx = moderate) or just gives OTC antihistamine (= low). Top gap: identify the actual medication name — that's the diagnosis itself, currently undocumented.
